rxn_class_summary: Anodic oxidation of 4,4'-dimethoxystilbene and substituted stilbenes to give tetraaryltetrahydrofurans, dehydrotetralins, aldehydes, indanyl acetamides, and pallidol/ampelopsin F-type products via radical cation dimerization
electrochemical_role: Anodic one-electron oxidation of the stilbene alkene to a radical cation, which dimerizes to a stable dication; subsequent nucleophilic capture (H2O, MeOH, AcOH, AcO-, MeCN) and intramolecular cyclization steps are non-electrochemical

key_intermediates.name: open-chain dication 31 from radical cation dimerization (threo 31a and meso 31b)
hydroxylated benzylic cation 32 (from H2O attack on 31)
spirocyclic carbocation 33 (ring-closed onto OH; precursor to aldehydes 1e/1f)
spirocyclic carbocation 35 (dearomatized; precursor to dehydrotetralins via 1,2-shift)
methoxylated spirocyclic cation 36 (MeOH analog of 33; precursor to acetals 1g/1h)
open-chain cation 37 (alkyl stilbene dication for stilbene 17)
indanyl cation (from cation 35-type cyclization in alkyl stilbenes)

electron_flow: The anode removes one electron from the stilbene to give a radical cation (30), which dimerizes through C-C bond formation at the β-carbons to a stable dication (31); nucleophiles (H2O, MeOH, AcO-, MeCN) then attack 31 or its rearranged spirocyclic cations (33, 35, 36) to deliver tetrahydrofurans, acetals, aldehydes, dehydrotetralins, or acetamides after deprotonation.
rate_determining_step: Radical cation dimerization of 30 to dication 31 (per Steckhan's kinetic studies)

probe_reagents.name: added water (1% H2O in MeCN/LiClO4)
probe_reagents.observation: Traps dication 31 to give tetrahydrofurans 1a/1b as major products, confirming dication is the primary electrolysis product

probe_reagents.name: nonaqueous workup (SiO2/CH2Cl2)
probe_reagents.observation: Markedly reduced tetrahydrofuran yield and increased dehydrotetralin yield, showing THFs arise from H2O attack during workup

probe_reagents.name: methanol (Steckhan's conditions, 25% MeOH/CH2Cl2)
probe_reagents.observation: Gives dimethyl acetals 1g/1h as major products via methoxylated spirocation 36, supporting spirocyclic intermediate

probe_reagents.name: sodium acetate / AcOH (Eberson's conditions)
probe_reagents.observation: AcO- captures the radical cation 30 before dimerization, giving acetates 1i/1j (62%)

probe_reagents.name: differentially disubstituted stilbenes (4-OMe vs 4'-CF3, 4'-NMe2 etc.)
probe_reagents.observation: Aryl migration in unsymmetrical stilbenes rules out open-chain carbocation; supports spirocyclic intermediate. Stronger donor placement determines regiochemistry of THF and dication coupling, supporting radical cation dimerization model

probe_reagents.name: X-ray diffraction on 1a, 1b, 1c, 1h, 1i, 1j, 2d, 6d, 14k, 15k, 16k, 20r, 21p, 22p, 22s, 23p, 23s, 25p
probe_reagents.observation: Unambiguous configurational assignment of products supporting the proposed pathways

probe_reagents.name: acetonitrile solvent (Ritter-type capture)
probe_reagents.observation: Traps indanyl cation in alkyl-substituted stilbenes 17-20 to give indanyl acetamides and tetralinyl acetamides (20q/20r), supporting cationic cyclization pathway

notes: Favored mechanism: one-electron anodic oxidation -> radical cation 30 -> radical cation dimerization to dication 31 -> nucleophile/intramolecular trapping. Alternative pathway via 1,2-aryl shifts in an open-chain carbocation 32 (Scheme 2, path a) was initially proposed for aldehyde 1e but ruled out by results on unsymmetrically substituted stilbenes, which require a spirocyclic cation (33/35/36) for the observed aryl migrations. Two cation-radical coupling modes (threo 31a, C2; meso 31b) explain the two THF diastereomers 1a/1b. Two ring-expansion pathways from spirocation 35 (1,2-p-methoxybenzyl shift -> 1c; 1,2-p-methoxystyryl shift -> 1d) compete, with the styryl shift preferred ~5:1 for stilbene 1. Competing radical cation capture (vs. dimerization) operates in the presence of strong nucleophiles (NaOAc). For alkyl/EWG-substituted stilbenes, open-chain dication and acetonitrile (Ritter) trapping become competitive.
